speaker: patient
text: Hi, doctor. I've been having blurred vision for the past couple of days—especially when reading or driving at night.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with a quick history and physical. Any other symptoms—fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, or changes in your skin or hair?

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your symptoms and initial labs, I’m diagnosing you with hypothyroidism. We’ll confirm with TSH and free T4, but this fits clinically.

speaker: patient
text: Hypothyroidism? That makes sense—I’ve felt unusually tired and my skin’s been dry lately. But I’m also worried about the blurry vision.

speaker: doctor
text: Good point—we’ll investigate that further. Blurred vision can be multifactorial, especially with endocrine conditions. Let’s check your vitals and run some urgent labs.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. I did notice my vision got worse after I skipped breakfast yesterday—I felt shaky and sweaty too.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fingerstick glucose just came back at 14.0 mmol/L—significantly elevated. That’s concerning and could explain the visual blurring due to osmotic lens changes.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is 37.2 °C—within normal limits—and your pulse is regular at 78 bpm.

speaker: doctor
text: Blood pressure is 128 over 69 mmHg—diastolic is on the lower side, but not alarming yet.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also had some mild nausea and increased thirst over the last 36 hours. Is that related?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—it’s consistent with hyperglycemia. We’ll need to rule out new-onset diabetes or thyroid-related glucose dysregulation. Let’s get HbA1c and fasting insulin next.

speaker: patient
text: I started taking levothyroxine two days ago—50 mcg—but I haven’t noticed much change yet.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s expected—levothyroxine takes weeks to stabilize. For now, we’ll focus on your glucose. I’d like to start you on basal insulin temporarily while we clarify the picture.

speaker: patient
text: Will that help with the blurry vision? It’s making it hard to work.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—once glucose normalizes, the lens swelling should resolve, usually within days. In the meantime, avoid driving if vision is significantly impaired.

speaker: patient
text: I took my temperature at home this morning—it was higher than usual. Around 38.4, actually.

speaker: doctor
text: Hmm—you’re right—I just rechecked: it’s 38.4 °C now. That’s a fever we didn’t see earlier. Let’s listen to your lungs and check for infection—this could be contributing to the glucose spike and worsening symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: I do have a sore throat and some ear fullness—I thought it was just a cold.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the fever, pharyngeal erythema, and elevated glucose, I’m concerned about an acute infection stressing your system. We’ll add a rapid strep test and consider empiric antibiotics.

speaker: patient
text: Just checked my glucose again with my home monitor—it’s still 14.0 mmol/L, same as that reading from earlier today.
